coredata.dc:title: Sulfur-doped carbon dots derived from cabbage leaves for the determination of alizarin
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Zhifeng
Meng, Guiyu
Li, Yanan
Zhou, Yingjie
coredata.dc:description: Sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CQDs) in the light of cabbage leaves were suggested through using a one-pot hydrothermal strategy. The cysteine was used as a sulfur dopant. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) suggested that the morphology of S-CQDs was highly dispersed and the particle size distribution range was located at 3.2–3.8 nm. Four elements (C, N, O and S) could be observed through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Some oxygen-containing and amino-containing groups could also be indicated by using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The S-CQDs suggested bright blue fluorescence under UV light. At the same time, the S-CQDs displayed exceptional anti bleaching property and salt resistance under UV light and NaCl solution, respectively. Moreover, the S-CQDs could be applied as excellent fluorescent nanoprobes to detect alizarin based on the fluorescence quenching strategy. The linear detection range was 0.5–100 μM with the detection limit of 0.14 μM. The fluorescence quenching principle was thoroughly studied based on the related characterizations. The behavior of the detection platform was studied in actual samples by using tap water, Yili milk and iced black tea samples. Finally, the S-CQDs could be applied for the sensing of temperature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cabbage leaves
Carbon dots
Fluorescent nanoprobe
alizarin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of alizarin in actual sample
S-CQDs for fluorescent measurement of alizarin
Research on possible fluorescence quenching mechanism
Measurement of alizarin in actual samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to thank the College Students\u2019 Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2438 ) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of S-CQDs. (B) Size distribution obtained from the TEM image (changing from 3.2 nm to 3.8 nm).
XPS spectrum (A) and single XPS spectra of: S2p (B), C1s (C), N1s (D) and O1s (E) of S-CQDs.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of S-CQDs under various excitation wavelength (340–390 nm). (B) The optimal fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of S-CQDs (inset: the picture of S-CQDs solution under sunlight (left) and UV light (right)).
The fluorescence responses of S-CQDs in the presence of different amount of alizarin. (B) The linear fitting curve between F 0/F and alizarin concentration (0.5–100 μM).
The alizarin measurement in actual samples.

head.ce:title: Sulfur-doped carbon dots derived from cabbage leaves for the determination of alizarin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong, 030619, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong, 030619, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CQDs) in the light of cabbage leaves were suggested through using a one-pot hydrothermal strategy. The cysteine was used as a sulfur dopant. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) suggested that the morphology of S-CQDs was highly dispersed and the particle size distribution range was located at 3.2–3.8 nm. Four elements (C, N, O and S) could be observed through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Some oxygen-containing and amino-containing groups could also be indicated by using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The S-CQDs suggested bright blue fluorescence under UV light. At the same time, the S-CQDs displayed exceptional anti bleaching property and salt resistance under UV light and NaCl solution, respectively. Moreover, the S-CQDs could be applied as excellent fluorescent nanoprobes to detect alizarin based on the fluorescence quenching strategy. The linear detection range was 0.5–100 μM with the detection limit of 0.14 μM. The fluorescence quenching principle was thoroughly studied based on the related characterizations. The behavior of the detection platform was studied in actual samples by using tap water, Yili milk and iced black tea samples. Finally, the S-CQDs could be applied for the sensing of temperature.

ce:section.ce:para: Alizarin is a typical mediator dye, and its scientific name is 1,2-Dihydroxy-9,10-anthraquinone. In 1868, the alizarin was synthesized through using anthracene extracted from coal tar by German chemists Greebe and Lieberman [1,2]. In industry, it is prepared by jointly heating anthraquinone-β-sulfur, caustic soda and potassium chlorate or potassium nitrate. In addition, alizarin can be also extracted from the roots of madder [3,4]. In terms of application, alizarin can be used as a drop reagent for aluminum, indium, mercury, zinc and zirconium and a staining agent for nerve tissue and live staining of protozoa [5]. Moreover, alizarin has an inhibitory effect on the growth of Staphylococcus aureus and can suppress the permeability of rat skin connective tissue. Lick rutin, it has anti-inflammatory effects [6]. Therefore, the fast and efficacious sensing and determination of alizarin in our lives is becoming increasingly important. To our best knowledge, the only method to detect alizarin was surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7,8]. Although these strategies could provide certain effective information, some defects limited its widespread use, such as complex operational procedures and expensive instruments [9]. Consequently, a facile, cost-effective and fast sensing strategy is significantly needed to detect alizarin. With the development of technology, the fluorescence sensing method has gained some focus for the substance testing due to eminent sensitivity, high simplicity and easy operation [10–12].

As a novel type of nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) are typically considered as standard spherical nanoparticles with a diameter of less than 10 nanometers and composed of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons and various surface functional groups [13–16]. Due to their excellent physical and chemical properties and good biocompatibility, CDs have been applied in various fields, such as chemical probes, biosensors, drug carriers, targeted markers and optoelectronic devices, which open up broad application prospects in multiple scientific fields including chemistry, physics, environment and biology [17–20]. The synthesis methods of CDs can be mainly divided into two categories: top-down and bottom-up method [21]. The top-down method is the process of decomposing larger carbon materials into nanoscale carbon particles, such as laser ablation method, electrochemical oxidation method, etc. Furthermore, the bottom-up method is the condensation of small molecule organic compounds or polymers into CDs through high-temperature carbonization, surface passivation and modification. This strategy includes hydrothermal synthesis, microwave pyrolysis and acid-base neutralization exothermic method [22,23]. The precursor sources for preparing CDs were very diverse. In fact, natural products have some advantages over other carbon sources, such as low cost, high abundance and the ability to be synthesized on a large scale. Nature provided almost unlimited resources, inspiring scientists to develop more novel carbon materials with minimal environmental impact. Compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic fluorescent groups, biobased CDs had superior performance. For example, they were widely used due to theri easy synthesis, good biocompatibility and excellent optical properties in biological imaging and sensing applications [24–26].

Herein, the S-CQDs with blue fluorescence were developed by using a facile one-pot hydrothermal strategy, in which the biomass cabbage leaves were applied as the carbon source and the cysteine was used as a sulfur dopant. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) suggested the morphology of S-CQDs, and the particle size distribution range was located at 3.2–3.8 nm. Four elements could be obtained through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Moreover, the S-CQDs displayed bright blue fluorescence under UV light. Meanwhile, the anti-bleaching property and salt resistance were discussed under different conditions. Additionally, the S-CQDs could be applied as excellent fluorescent nanoprobes to detect alizarin based on the fluorescence quenching strategy. The linear detection range was 0.5–100 μM with the detection limit of 0.14 μM. The fluorescence quenching principle was thoroughly studied due to the related characterizations. The behavior of the detection platform was studied in actual samples by using tap water, Yili milk and iced black tea samples.

Sodium chloride (99.5%), potassium chloride (99.5%), calcium chloride (AR), ferric chloride (AR), copper chloride (AR), zinc chloride (AR), potassium dichromate (≥99.5%), magnesium chloride (99%), sodium carbonate (≥99.5%), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (99%), disodium hydrogen phosphate (99%), sodium citrate (99%), citric acid (99.5%), hydrochloric acid (37%), sodium hydroxide (AR), vancomycin (≥80%), catechins (≥97%), tyramine (98%), isoniazid (98%), proline (99%), tryptophan (99%), cysteine (99%), glucose (≥99.5%), ascorbic acid (AR), VB6 (≥98%), vanillic aldehyde (≥99%), xylosic alcohol (≥98%) and urea (99%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Alizarin (97%) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The cabbage leaves were acquired from Tangjiu convenience store. The raw reagents were used directly without any purification.

The morphology and size of S-CQDs were discussed on a JEM-2100 type transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) with a voltage of 200 kV. A Nicolet 5700 type Fourier transform infrared spectroscope (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific, USA) was adopted to analyze types of functional groups of S-CQDs. The testing method was KBr pellet technique, and the scope of recording was 4000–500 cm−1. A UV-4500 type ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Hitachi, Japan) was carried out to investigate the UV–vis spectrum of S-CQDs. The fluorescence measurements were recorded on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with the excitation/emission silts of 10/10 nm. A FLS980 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, UK) was applied to discuss the fluorescence lifetime of S-CQDs without and with the addition of alizarin. The types and contents of elements in S-CQDs were studied by using an ESCALAB 250Xi type X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific, USA).

Herein, the hydrothermal strategy was applied for the development of S-CQDs. First of all, the cabbage leaves were cleaned multiple times with ultrapure water and acetone. Then, the cabbage leaves were dried at 50 °C for 24 h and crushed through a mortar. In the synthesis of S-CQDs, cabbage leaf powder of 2 g and cysteine powder of 0.1 g were dropped into a beaker containing 30 mL ultrapure water. Then, a magnetic stirrer was used to disperse the mixture at room temperature for 30 min. Next, the dispersion liquid was transferred into a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor and maintained at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product solution was centrifuged by using a centrifuge at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The voltage and excitation and emission slits of the F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer were carried out to be 400 V and 10/10 nm, respectively. The detection process was conducted at room temperature. The fluorescence analysis of alizarin based on the S-CQDs was displayed as following procedures. First of all, the S-CQDs solution of 100 μL was dropped into the buffer solution of 1.9 mL (disodium hydrogen phosphate-citric acid based buffer solution, pH=6). Then, the fluorescence emission data were obtained under the excitation wavelength of 369 nm. Next, the linear range and detection limit (LOD) were fitted and calculated in light of the above fluorescence data. Finally, the selectivity experiments were discussed under the same conditions. The reference materials had Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, CO3 2−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cr6+, ascorbic acid, VB6, vanillic aldehyde, xylosic alcohol, sodium citrate, citric acid, vancomycin, catechins, tyramine, isoniazid, proline, tryptophan, cysteine, glucose and urea (the concentration was 300 μM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of alizarin in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: To discuss the practical applicability of S-CQDs for the sensing of alizarin in real samples, the S-CQDs probe was carried out to accurately determine alizarin in tap, Yili milk and iced black tea. The tap water samples were collected from laboratory. Then the water samples were centrifuged and filtered to remove the potential suspended particles. The Yili milk and iced black tea samples were purchased from Jinhu convenience store. These samples were diluted 10 times by the disodium hydrogen phosphate-citric acid based buffer solution (pH=6). Next, the spiked tests were carried out through some corresponding operations and the fluorescence data were recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer. Finally, the recovery rate and relative standard deviation (RSD) were obtained through processing experimental data.

The morphology of S-CQDs was discussed via using TEM analysis. It could be clearly seen from Fig. 1 A, the morphology of S-CQDs presented a spherical shape, and it had excellent dispersibility. The particle size histogram was displayed in Fig. 1B. The particle size changed from 3.2 nm to 3.8 nm, and the average value was 3.5 nm. The crystal structure of S-CQDs was studied through using XRD analytical method. The XRD crystal diagram was suggested in Fig. S1. A broad peak around 21.5° was ascribed to the (100) crystal plane of carbon. The XRD result indicated that the crystal form of S-CQDs was amorphous [27,28]. The FT-IR analysis method was applied to investigate the surface functional groups of S-CQDs. From Fig. S2, some stretching vibration characteristic peaks could be observed, such as 3445.7 cm−1 (O-H or N-H bond), 2969.8 and 2877.3 cm−1 (C-H bond), 1632.9 cm−1 (C=O bond), 1388.1 cm−1 (C-N bond) and 1112.2 cm−1 (C-O bond) [29,30]. Meanwhile, some bending vibration characteristic peaks could also be obtained including 1353.7 cm−1 (C-H bond) and 928.4 cm−1 (O-H bond) [31]. Next, the elemental composition and content in S-CQDs were investigated on the XPS characterization. Four characteristic peaks could be observed in Fig. 2 A, including C1s (285.1 eV), N1s (399.4 eV), O1s (532.5 eV) and S2p (164.2 eV). The contents of C, N, O and S were 74.98%, 12%, 9.74% and 3.29%, respectively. Six characteristic peaks, such as S2p3/2 (163.5 eV), S2p1/2 (164.6 eV), S-O (165.9 eV), C-SO2 (167.2 eV), C-SO3 (167.7 eV) and C-SO4 (169.2 eV), could be obtained in the single XPS spectrum of S2p (Fig. 2B) [32,33]. Two characteristic peaks, such as C-C/C=C (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286.2 eV), could be obtained in the single XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2C) [34]. The single XPS of N1s fitting spectrum (Fig. 2D) implied that two feature peaks were observed, such as pyridinic N (399.1 eV) and pyrrolic N (401.2 eV) [35]. As displayed in Fig. 2E, the particularity peaks of single XPS spectrum of O1s suggested that the peaks at 531.5 eV and 532.6 eV were attributed to the C=O and C-O bonds, respectively [36]. The XPS result suggested that the S has been successfully doped into the as-prepared CDs, and these active functional groups were the basis for S-CQDs to have good water solubility.

The effect of the excitation wavelength on the emission wavelength was investigated. It could be clearly seen from Fig. 3 A, when the excitation wavelength increased from 340 nm to 390 nm, the emission wavelength suggested a redshift phenomenon. This change phenomenon implied that the S-CQDs had excitation-dependent particularity, which might be assigned to be the complex surface states and particle size of S-CQDs. The optimum excitation and emission wavelengths were 369 nm and 444 nm by continuously adjusting the excitation and emission wavelengths (Fig. 3B). The S-CQDs emitted blue fluorescence under UV light (inset in Fig. 3B). Moreover, the QY value was calculated to be 15.7% via utilizing quinine sulfate as the reference. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of S-CQDs was displayed in Fig. S3. The peaks around 259 nm and 320 nm were assigned to the π-π* and n-π* transition of C=O and nitrogen-containing unsaturated bonds, respectively. It was thus clear that the nitrogen was doped into the S-CQDs.

Next, the fluorescence intensities of S-CQDs were explored under various conditions, such as ultraviolet irradiation time, pH value and NaCl solution. From Fig. S4A, with ultraviolet irradiation time increasing from 0 min to 2 min, a faint weakening phenomenon was discovered for the fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence quenching rate was only 0.22%. This result implied that the S-CQDs had prime photostability. The influence of pH value on the fluorescence intensity was also researched. As indicated in Fig. S4B, a broad peak could be observed at the pH of 6. Apparently, the fluorescence intensity was relatively weak in the presence of acid or alkali. This phenomenon of change was assigned to the protonation and deprotonation. The fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs was also discussed under various NaCl concentration. The fluorescence intensity remained very stable when the NaCl concentration reached 0.05 M (Fig. S4C). The prime stability under different ultraviolet irradiation time and NaCl amount (except for pH value) suggested that the S-CQDs had high application value as fluorescence probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: S-CQDs for fluorescent measurement of alizarin
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility and sensitivity of alizarin by using S-CQDs was discussed. It could be clearly seen from Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence emission spectra of the measurement solution based on S-CQDs were obtained with the alizarin amount changing from 0 μM to 300 μM. When the alizarin amount varied from 0 to 300 μM, the intensity of sensing platform around 444 nm suggested a downward trend. At the same time, it also indicated the fluorescence reduction degree was proportional to the amount of alizarin with an excellent linear regular pattern, implying that the S-CQDs could be applied as fluorescence nanosensors for the measurement of alizarin. We used as F 0/F as longitudinal coordinates and alizarin amount as horizontal ordinate to develop a linear curve. As implied in Fig. 4B, a prime linear relationship between F 0/F and alizarin amount was suggested with a concentration range of 0.5–100 μM, and the linear equation was fitted to be F 0/F=0.11[Q]+1.01 with a fitting coefficient of 0.9955 (F 0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensity of sensing system without and with the addition of alizarin. Q is the alizarin concentration). Moreover, the detection limit (LOD) of 0.14 μM was obtained through relevant calculations. Compared with other methods, this system possessed some advantages including low cost, facile operation and lower toxicity (Table S1). Furthermore, the selectivity experiment was displayed in Fig. S5. Except for Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, CO3 2−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cr6+, ascorbic acid, VB6, vanillic aldehyde, xylosic alcohol, sodium citrate, citric acid, vancomycin, catechins, tyramine, isoniazid, proline, tryptophan, cysteine, glucose and urea, only alizarin showed a significant weakening of the fluorescence of S-CQDs. This result indicated that the S-CQDs had excellent selectivity for the alizarin measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on possible fluorescence quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
The potential fluorescence quenching principle of S-CQDs for the sensing of alizarin was discussed. Some related characterization data were obtained, such as UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra and fluorescence lifetime. It could be clearly seen from Fig. S6A, there was an overlap phenomenon between the excitation band of S-CQDs and the absorption band of alizarin. To our knowledge, the extraordinary overlap phenomenon between the excitation or emission spectra of sensors and the UV–vis absorption spectra of analytes was a fundamental element for the existence of inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism [37]. The existence of IFE required further confirmation from the change of fluorescence lifetime. The fluorescence lifetime spectra of S-CQDs in the absence and presence of alizarin were displayed in Fig. S6B. The fluorescence lifetime value of S-CQDs without and with addition of alizarin were 4.16 ns and 4.13 ns by processing relevant data. No apparent change of fluorescence lifetime was observed after the addition of alizarin in the S-CQDs solution. Therefore, the IFE might be the fluorescence quenching mechanism of S-CQDs for the sensing of alizarin [38]. The Parker Eq. (1) was employed to further confirm the mechanism.
(1)
F
c o r
F
o b s d
=
2.3 d
A

e m
2.3 s

e m
1 −
10
− s
A
e m

The absorbance values (A ex and A em) of S-CQDs at 369 nm and 444 nm, the width of the cuvette (d), the distance between the excitation beam edges (g), the cuvette edge and the width of the excitation beam (s) were calculated to obtain the corrected and measured fluorescence intensities ratio (F cor/F obsd). As shown in Table S2, when the concentration of the introduced alizarin increased from 0 μM to 50 μM, the Fcor/Fobsd value raised from 1.12 to 1.57, demonstrating the quench mechanism was consistent with IFE effect [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of alizarin in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The actual samples were mixed with the standard solution of alizarin for spiked recovery rate tests to discuss the practicability of the fluorescence detection platform based on S-CQDs. The alizarin measurement results were displayed in Table 1 . The recoveries of 96.7–108.6% in tap water, 96.0–108.4% in Yili milk and 92.3–107.0% in iced black tea were obtained separately. The RSD value changed from 1.04% to 4.86% in tap water, Yili milk and iced black tea samples. In this study, alizarin was also quantified by a HPLC analysis method, and the findings were compared with sensor results. The recovery percentages for HPLC were found to vary from 96.4% to 110.7%. The RSD was lower than 3.57%. The effective recovery of the alizarin demonstrated that the probe had promising application for determination of alizarin in real samples.

The feasibility of temperature sensing through using S-CQDs was discussed. It could be clearly seen from Fig. S7A, the fluorescence emission spectra of the S-CQDs were obtained under various temperature. When the temperature varied from 20 °C to 100°C, the intensity around 444 nm suggested a downward trend. We used F as longitudinal coordinates and temperature as horizontal ordinate to develop a linear curve. As implied in Fig. S7B, a prime linear relationship between F and temperature was suggested with a range of 20–70°C, and the linear equation was fitted to be F=-26.7Tem+5228 with a fitting coefficient of 0.99. Finally, this detection platform could be cycled several times between 20 °C and 70°C, and its fluorescence intensity was still not significantly different from the initial value (Fig. S7C).

In conclusion, the blue fluorescence S-CQDs with exceptional optical performance were fabricated through hydrothermal strategy utilizing cabbage leaves and cysteine as the carbon and sulfur sources. The S-CQDs displayed highly dispersed, fascinating stability and excitation-dependent fluorescence property. As a result of the IFE quenching effect, the S-CQDs were applied as fluorescent nanosensors for the determination of alizarin. This nanosensor held excellent selectivity and sensitivity, and was used to determine alizarin in real samples. The results of spiked recoveries and RSD value were satisfactory. This platform indicated remarkable application value for the measurement of alizarin in complex samples. Finally, the S-CQDs could be applied for the sensing of temperature.
Data will be made available on request.

Zhifeng Cai: Validation, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Guiyu Meng: Software, Conceptualization, Supervision. Yanan Li: Software, Conceptualization, Supervision. Yingjie Zhou: Software, Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the College Students’ Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2438) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.141247.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at xxxxxxxxxxxx.
